questions.prompt: What's the key difference between Tailwind utilities and inline styles?

choices.text: Inline styles can't do hover, focus, dark mode, media queries, group/peer states; can't compose into a design system; have higher specificity that breaks cascade; can't be tree-shaken. Tailwind utilities are CSS classes generated from a config — they support pseudo-classes, media queries, animations, AND enforce a design system scale (spacing, colors, type sizes). The constraint matters: Tailwind's defaults nudge you toward consistency. You can escape via arbitrary values (`p-[17px]`) but the friction keeps the design system honest.

questions.explanation: Utilities support pseudo-classes/media/dark-mode AND enforce design-system constraints. Inline styles do neither.

questions.prompt: You're copying the same long Tailwind class string across 8 places. What's the right abstraction?

choices.text: Use @apply to extract a .button CSS class.

Extract a React (or Vue/Svelte) COMPONENT, not a CSS class. The right unit of reuse in Tailwind is the component — `<Button variant='primary'>` lives in one file with its classes inline. @apply has subtle problems: defeats Tailwind's purging logic, hides design intent, encourages CSS-class thinking that loses utility-first benefits. Component extraction keeps classes co-located with the component, supports props and variants cleanly (especially via class-variance-authority), and refactors naturally. The Tailwind team's official guidance is the same: prefer components over @apply.

questions.explanation: Components are the Tailwind reuse unit. @apply hides intent and fights the utility-first model.
